title: Quiz: Molecular Orbitals
description: Test your understanding of molecular orbital theory, LCAO, and bond order calculations

questions.question: What is the bond order of O₂ according to molecular orbital theory?
questions.explanation: O₂ has 16 electrons. Filling the MO diagram: 10 electrons in bonding orbitals, 6 in antibonding. Bond Order = (10-6)/2 = 2. This is a double bond.

questions.question: Which statement best describes a bonding molecular orbital?

options.text: Higher energy than the constituent atomic orbitals with a node between nuclei
Lower energy than the constituent atomic orbitals with electron density concentrated between nuclei
Same energy as the constituent atomic orbitals
Only formed from p orbitals

questions.explanation: Bonding orbitals result from constructive interference of atomic orbitals, concentrating electron density between nuclei. This increased electron density stabilizes the molecule, lowering the energy.

questions.question: Why is O₂ paramagnetic while N₂ is diamagnetic?

options.text: O₂ has more electrons than N₂
O₂ has unpaired electrons in degenerate π* orbitals
N₂ has a triple bond while O₂ has a double bond
O₂ has more antibonding electrons

questions.explanation: O₂ has 2 electrons in the degenerate π*2p orbitals. Following Hund's rule, they occupy separate orbitals with parallel spins, giving 2 unpaired electrons. N₂ has all electrons paired.

questions.question: In LCAO-MO theory, combining two atomic orbitals produces:

options.text: One molecular orbital
Two molecular orbitals of equal energy
Two molecular orbitals: one bonding, one antibonding
Three molecular orbitals

questions.explanation: Conservation of orbitals: N atomic orbitals combine to form N molecular orbitals. Two atomic orbitals yield two MOs—one bonding (lower energy) and one antibonding (higher energy).

questions.question: A σ (sigma) bond has cylindrical symmetry around the internuclear axis.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True! σ orbitals have no nodal planes containing the bond axis, giving them cylindrical symmetry. This is in contrast to π orbitals, which have a nodal plane through the axis.

questions.question: What is the bond order of He₂?
questions.explanation: He₂ has 4 electrons: 2 in σ1s (bonding) and 2 in σ*1s (antibonding). Bond Order = (2-2)/2 = 0. This is why helium doesn't form a stable diatomic molecule.

questions.question: In a heteronuclear diatomic molecule like HF, the bonding MO has more electron density on the more electronegative atom.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True! In heteronuclear molecules, the more electronegative atom contributes more to the bonding orbital. In HF, the bonding MO has more F character, explaining the polar nature of the bond.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describe π (pi) molecular orbitals? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Formed from sideways overlap of p orbitals
Have a nodal plane containing the internuclear axis
Are always lower in energy than σ orbitals
Come in degenerate pairs (πx and πy)

questions.explanation: a) Correct: π bonds form from parallel p orbitals overlapping sideways. b) Correct: π orbitals have a nodal plane through the bond axis. c) Incorrect: π can be higher or lower than σ depending on the molecule. d) Correct: πx and πy are degenerate in diatomics.
